text.title: Content Audit — Real Findings Examples
text.summary: Concrete audit findings for blog and help center content, demonstrating action assignment logic.

example.note: Finding: 40% of blog posts are 3+ years old. Screenshots show old UI, pricing information is outdated, links are broken. Action: Update top 20 posts by traffic; archive low-traffic posts over 3 years old. Reasoning: High-traffic old posts are worth updating (Update); low-traffic old posts have no recovery path (Remove).

antipattern.description: Marking all old posts as Remove without checking traffic.
antipattern.reason: High-traffic posts that rank well in search may still drive significant value despite age. Removing them loses that value; updating recovers it.

example.note: Finding: 15 articles cover similar topics with conflicting answers. Issues: user confusion, SEO cannibalization between pages, maintenance overhead. Action: Consolidate into 5 comprehensive guides, redirect the 10 removed URLs. Reasoning: Overlapping coverage with conflicting information is the textbook Consolidate case.

antipattern.description: Assigning Update to all 15 duplicate articles individually.
antipattern.reason: Updating duplicates preserves the fragmentation and SEO cannibalization; consolidation is the correct action when multiple pieces cover the same topic.

rule.statement: When two or more content items cover the same topic, assign Consolidate — not Update — to all of them, then create a single canonical piece.
rule.rationale: Updating duplicates separately leaves the duplication problem intact and increases maintenance burden.

rule.statement: Assign Remove only when the content has no recovery path: topic is obsolete, traffic is negligible, and no redirect target exists.
rule.rationale: Premature removal of low-traffic content that covers unique topics removes search entry points without benefit.
